questions.question: Match each type of apraxia to its description:
categories: Cannot voluntarily perform learned actions on command

correct_mapping.Ideomotor apraxia: Cannot voluntarily perform learned actions on command

questions.explanation: Ideomotor apraxia = can't voluntarily pantomime actions (but may do them automatically), ideational apraxia = can't correctly sequence complex multi-step actions, verbal apraxia = can't coordinate speech motor movements.

questions.question: Match each visual stream to its characteristics:
categories: Where/how pathway — guides action, runs to parietal cortex
What pathway — object identification, runs to temporal cortex

correct_mapping.Dorsal stream: Where/how pathway — guides action, runs to parietal cortex
correct_mapping.Ventral stream: What pathway — object identification, runs to temporal cortex

questions.explanation: The dorsal stream (occipital → parietal) is the 'where/how' action-guidance pathway, while the ventral stream (occipital → temporal) is the 'what' object-identification pathway. This was characterised by Goodale and Milner.

questions.question: Match each patient/lesion pattern to the resulting deficit:
categories: Can't describe object orientation but can use mail slot
Can name objects but can't reach/grasp them

correct_mapping.Temporal lobe damage: Can't describe object orientation but can use mail slot
correct_mapping.Parietal lobe damage: Can name objects but can't reach/grasp them

questions.explanation: Temporal damage impairs conscious visual perception but spares action (mail slot task preserved). Parietal damage impairs action but spares identification. Patient R.V. specifically showed impaired precision grip due to parietal damage.

questions.question: Match each motor area to its specialisation:
questions.explanation: Each area has a distinct specialisation: SMA for self-initiated sequences, premotor for externally guided movements, M1 for executing motor commands, and parietal cortex for integrating sensory information to coordinate movements.

questions.question: Match each cerebellar damage location to its effect:
categories: General ataxia affecting posture and gait
Intention tremor of ipsilateral voluntary movements

correct_mapping.Medial vermis damage: General ataxia affecting posture and gait
correct_mapping.Lateral cerebellar damage: Intention tremor of ipsilateral voluntary movements

questions.explanation: Medial vermis damage causes general ataxia (balance, posture, gait problems), while lateral cerebellar damage causes intention tremor affecting ipsilateral voluntary movements — the tremor worsens as the limb approaches a target.

questions.question: Match each learning concept to its description:
categories: Striatum required for motor skill acquisition

correct_mapping.Impaired in BG disorders: Striatum required for motor skill acquisition

questions.explanation: Motor learning includes sequence learning (SRT) and adaptation. Being preserved in amnesia shows it doesn't need the hippocampus; being impaired in BG disorders confirms the striatum's critical role in acquiring motor skills.

questions.question: Match each mirror neuron property to its description:
categories: Fires for both performing and observing
More widely distributed than in monkeys

correct_mapping.Dual activation: Fires for both performing and observing
correct_mapping.Human mirror system: More widely distributed than in monkeys

questions.explanation: Mirror neurons show dual activation (perform + observe), context specificity (different responses depending on the goal), are found in F5 and inferior parietal in monkeys, and form a more widely distributed network in humans.

questions.question: Match each hierarchy level to its role:
questions.explanation: The motor hierarchy: upper motor neurons specify commands, basal ganglia gate initiation timing, cerebellum corrects errors during execution, and lower levels (spinal cord) provide the final output pathway and basic reflexes.

questions.question: Match each experiment to what it demonstrated:
categories: Putamen most active during novel learning

correct_mapping.Joystick 90-degree offset task: Putamen most active during novel learning

questions.explanation: Bernstein showed motor programs are abstract (signatures preserved across effectors), Tanji showed SMA encodes sequences, the dot motion paradigm showed graded sensory-motor conversion, and the joystick task showed BG are most active during novel learning.

questions.question: Match each motor learning phase to its characteristics:
categories: Broad activation, effortful, cerebellum most active

correct_mapping.Early phase: Broad activation, effortful, cerebellum most active

questions.explanation: Early motor learning involves broad cortical activation with heavy cerebellar involvement (error correction during learning). Advanced phase shows efficient, specific M1 activation as the skill becomes automatic.
